trajectory_summary: This is a duplicate-contact call — Gary had already spoken with another ION rep (Ella) and booked an appointment before Bucky reached him. Bucky ran a competent but mechanical verify pass, discovered the prior booking at 02:11, and exited without capturing any handoff context or reinforcing the appointment. The call produced no new value and left the closer with nothing Bucky learned. Energy was flat throughout and leaked at the close when Bucky apologized and disengaged rather than using the 90 seconds of qualify data he had just collected.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is listed as 'unknown.' The actual outcome is a duplicate contact on an already-booked lead — not a new booking, not a DQ, not a callback. The appointment exists but Bucky contributed nothing to its quality. The closer will walk in without the TPO roof detail, the 25-year-old panel replacement intent, the $200 PNM bill, or the sole-decision-maker status that Bucky collected and then discarded.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Duplicate-contact exit discipline — handoff context over clean escape
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Oh, then you're all good. Sorry to bother you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky collected six closer-relevant data points in under two minutes (TPO flat roof, 10 panels 25+ years old, PNM utility, ~$200/month bill, sole decision-maker, credit 670+, replacement intent) and then discarded all of it with an apology exit. The manager should use this call to establish what a duplicate-contact call is for: not to rebook, but to enrich the closer's context. The coaching move is to ask Bucky where Gary's TPO roof detail went after he hung up — and then build the habit of a 20-second confirmation-and-note close that makes the 90 seconds already spent worth something to the closer.

key_moments.quote: Hey, Gary. Sorry. Yeah. Sorry. This is Bucky with ION. I'm reaching out about the solar form you filled out online. How are you doing?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky opens with two apologies before he has said anything worth apologizing for. The 'Sorry. Yeah. Sorry.' stutter costs legitimacy in the first four seconds. The rest of the intro is structurally correct — company name, form reference, social opener — but the apology framing undercuts the calm, neighbor-like energy the codex targets.

key_moments.quote: Yes. I do. Okay. And price was fast.
key_moments.stewart_read: DIARIZATION CAVEAT: Deepgram assigns this line to speaker_1 (Bucky), but the content — confirming solar ownership and editorializing about panel speed — is semantically inconsistent with Bucky's role and consistent with Gary responding to 'do you already have solar?' at 00:40. If this is Gary's line, 'price was fast' is an unprompted cost-sensitivity signal that opens a direct path to the replacement conversation. Bucky's follow-up at 00:46 ('Are you looking to add on or replace or anything?') is a reasonable pivot regardless of attribution. The enthusiasm signal classification is flagged as diarization-dependent — the manager should verify the audio before using this moment in a 1-on-1.

key_moments.quote: I've got 10 that are twenty year over twenty five years old.
key_moments.stewart_read: Gary volunteers that he has 10 panels that are 25+ years old and is considering replacing them. This is a high-value closer signal — a customer with owned legacy equipment, replacement intent, and a cost-sensitivity lean is a warm lead for a full system upgrade conversation. Bucky captures the data point but does nothing to anchor it emotionally or signal its significance to Gary. The moment passes as a checkbox.

key_moments.quote: I just sent I sent a copy.
key_moments.stewart_read: Gary reveals he already sent his bill to another ION rep. Bucky's response — 'Oh, to who?' — is the right instinct (confirm the prior contact), but the follow-up at 02:11 ('did you get scheduled?') and the exit at 02:15 abandon everything Bucky just collected. The bill is on file, the appointment is booked, and Bucky has 90 seconds of qualify data that the closer will never see.
